engineering.translation: These bodies of the eternal, indestructible, immeasurable embodied are said to have an end. Therefore fight. The verse closes a four-verse argument with explicit license: act. Your codebase is mortal. Saying so out loud is not tragic; it is just true about implementations. What does not perish in the rewrite is your contract, your spec, the customer-need this service exists to satisfy. Therefore ship the migration. Tasmād yudhyasva carries an engineering license. Rewriting kills only what was always going to be killed — this framework, these files, those tests. Rewriting preserves what is actually load-bearing — the API your callers depend on, the invariant the business has agreed to honor. So the rewrite is not destruction. It is a body change. A senior who frames migration as 'killing my predecessor's work' has chosen the wrong word. Such code was already perishable. What survives is whatever was real about the system, and that survives by design.
engineering.concrete_scenario: A staff engineer at a mid-sized fintech stalls before kicking off a six-month rewrite: the existing payments service, authored by a now-departed team, has run in production for nine years on a Spring monolith with a custom Hibernate ORM layer. Twelve thousand lines of code, hundreds of integration tests, a deployment runbook nobody fully understands. Its replacement: a Go binary with explicit handlers, Kafka instead of in-process queues, Postgres logical replication for moving data. Our engineer keeps freezing because rewriting feels like 'killing my predecessor's work.' At an architecture review, the principal pulls up this verse: antavanta ime dehāḥ — these bodies have an end; the Spring service was always going to be replaced; what survived nine years was not the Hibernate hack but the contract — POST /v1/charges, idempotency-key semantics, fraud-rule precedence order, settlement reconciliation invariants. Those are nityasya śarīriṇaḥ — eternal in this chapter's specific sense: they outlive whatever currently implements them. Tasmād yudhyasva: therefore, do the rewrite. Our engineer is not killing predecessor work; that legacy code was already mortal. They are preserving whatever was real about it by granting it a fresh body. The principal hands them a rollback plan and tells them to begin dual-write phase on Monday.
engineering.falsifiability: The analog fails if a reader uses it to justify rewrites that are not actually needed — vanity migrations, framework chasing, 'this Rails app is fine but I'd rather it were in Rust.' Krishna does not license arbitrary action; he licenses Arjuna's specific prescribed duty (svadharma) when paralysis has set in. The engineering equivalent is a migration that is genuinely required: the old framework is unsupported, the database is at scale limits, the contract has been violated by the implementation drift. If you find yourself quoting 2.18 to start a rewrite that nobody asked for, you have inverted it; the verse explicitly couples action to dharma, not to engineering preference.
engineering.counter_example: When the right action is to NOT migrate — when the existing system is load-bearing, when the business has not asked for change, when the engineering cost exceeds any contract benefit — this verse does not contradict that. The opposite move (preserving the body because the body is, in this specific case, also serving the spec adequately) is not denied. Chapter 5 and Chapter 18 are where right-renunciation lives; this verse is specifically the license to act when paralysis has set in around an action that genuinely needs to happen.
engineering.implication: When you stall before a migration because rewriting feels like erasure, name what is actually mortal (the codebase, the framework) and what is not (the contract, the invariant, the customer-need). The first is meant to die; the second cannot be killed by the migration. Then begin the migration.
engineering.quotable_line: The migration does not kill the system; it gives the system a new body. The system was always the contract.

iterations.mutation: v0 generation: BG 2.18 explicitly licenses action because the body is perishable. Engineering layer maps to rewrite/migration paralysis — the staff engineer who frames the rewrite as 'killing predecessor work' is using the wrong word. The codebase is the perishable body; the contract is the imperishable embodied. tasmād yudhyasva is the explicit license: ship the migration. Counter-example handles the symmetric failure (vanity migrations).
v0.1: Varied repeated nouns ('migration' / 'old' / 'code' / 'service') with synonyms (rewrite / legacy / predecessor work) to lift unique-token ratio above 0.55. No doctrinal change.
